text:
This technical note addresses the challenge of designing distributed consensus protocols for multi-agent systems with general linear dynamics operating under directed communication topologies. A significant limitation in current literature is that consensus protocols often rely on global knowledge of the communication graph—specifically the smallest real part of the nonzero eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix—to determine coupling weights. This reliance on global information prevents the protocols from being truly distributed.

To ensure fully distributed implementation, we propose a novel adaptive consensus protocol that requires only the agent dynamics and local relative state information from neighboring agents. We demonstrate that this protocol achieves leader-follower consensus if the communication graph contains a directed spanning tree rooted at the leader. A key innovation in our approach is the introduction of monotonically increasing functions within the adaptive laws, which provides the design flexibility necessary to handle the asymmetries inherent in directed graphs. We utilize a novel Lyapunov functions analysis to prove the stability of the system and show that the time-varying coupling weights converge to finite values. Furthermore, we extend the results to address the containment control problem for systems with multiple leaders. The existence of these adaptive protocols is guaranteed provided that the individual agent dynamics are stabilizable. Simulation results are provided to validate the effectiveness of the proposed design.
